member_a_label: Bacterial Canker And Wilt Of Tomato

graph.confusable_reason: Both are bacterial diseases of tomato that produce dark leaf spots and can occur together in cool, wet conditions; in early/foliar stages small necrotic specks and marginal leaf lesions look similar, and both are seed- and splash-borne, so growers frequently confuse them before systemic or fruit signs develop.
graph.shared_presentation: Small dark brown-to-black necrotic leaf lesions, leaflet marginal scorching, foliar blighting, and fruit blemishes; both favored by cool wet weather and overhead irrigation, both seedborne and spread by water splash, and both can cause early-season leaf flecking that mimics the other.

forks.question: Is the disease systemic (vascular wilt) or strictly localized to spots/specks?
forks.a_signal: Progressive one-sided wilting, leaf curling and unilateral leaflet collapse indicating a systemic vascular infection
forks.b_signal: Strictly localized, discrete leaf and fruit specks with no wilting; plant remains turgid

forks.question: What is the size and halo of the leaf lesions?
forks.a_signal: Marginal leaf scorch and larger blotchy necrosis ('firing' of leaflet edges), bird's-eye fruit spots with raised white halo
forks.b_signal: Tiny (1-2 mm) dark brown-black specks, often with a narrow yellow chlorotic halo, scattered over leaflet and fruit surface

forks.question: What do the fruit lesions look like?
forks.a_signal: Bird's-eye spots: raised white halo surrounding a tan-brown center, slightly larger
forks.b_signal: Numerous tiny black superficial specks (under 1 mm), slightly sunken, never raised halo

forks.question: Does the plant decline and die from a wilt, or just accumulate cosmetic spots?
forks.a_signal: Plant progressively wilts and dies; petiole/stem cankers and cavities develop; yield loss from plant death
forks.b_signal: Plant survives; damage is cosmetic spotting and defoliation only, no plant death from wilt

forks.question: Cut the stem longitudinally near the soil line and inspect the vascular tissue and pith.
forks.a_signal: Yellow-to-reddish-brown vascular discoloration, mealy/disintegrating pith, internal cavities, and creamy bacterial ooze from the cut xylem (Clavibacter is systemic in the xylem)
forks.b_signal: Clean white vascular tissue with no discoloration, no cankers, no ooze (Pseudomonas speck never invades the vascular system)

graph.narrative_a: Bacterial canker (Clavibacter michiganensis ssp. michiganensis) is a systemic, xylem-invading bacterium. Early symptoms can mimic foliar bacterial diseases with leaf-margin necrosis ('firing'), but it characteristically causes one-sided wilting, leaflet curling, stem cankers and internal pith cavities, and yellow-brown vascular discoloration. Fruit show diagnostic 'bird's-eye' spots (tan center, raised white halo). It is highly destructive because infected plants commonly die. Splitting the stem reveals vascular browning and creamy bacterial streaming, the definitive sign.
graph.narrative_b: Bacterial speck (Pseudomonas syringae pv. tomato) is a foliar/superficial disease that produces tiny (1-2 mm) dark brown-to-black specks with narrow yellow halos on leaves, plus numerous tiny superficial black specks on fruit. It is strongly favored by cool (around 18-24 C), wet conditions. It is non-systemic: it never invades the vascular system, never causes wilt, cankers, or pith breakdown, and rarely kills the plant. Damage is cosmetic spotting, leaf blighting, and reduced fruit marketability.
graph.management_a: Use certified pathogen-free seed and transplants; hot-water or acid seed treatment. Rogue and destroy infected plants (systemic, not curable). Rotate 3+ years out of solanaceous crops, sanitize stakes/tools and greenhouse surfaces. Avoid overhead irrigation and working plants when wet. Copper sprays give only limited suppression of secondary spread; emphasis is on exclusion and sanitation.
graph.management_b: Plant pathogen-free certified seed (hot-water treat seed); avoid overhead irrigation and reduce leaf wetness duration. Apply preventive copper plus mancozeb (copper + EBDC synergy) on a protective schedule, especially during cool wet periods; rotate copper chemistries and monitor for copper resistance. Rotate out of solanaceous crops and incorporate/destroy crop debris. Because it is cosmetic and non-lethal, focus is on protecting fruit quality during favorable weather windows.

lay.farmer_lay_report: My tomato plants have small dark brown-to-black spots on the leaves, and the leaf edges are browning and looking scorched, with some overall leaf die-back and a few blemishes on the fruit. It got worse during the cool wet spell and after overhead watering.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Cut the stem lengthwise: look for a yellow-to-reddish-brown stained streak running up the inside of the stem, a dry mealy or crumbly center, hollow pockets inside, and a creamy white ooze that seeps out of the cut. The plant also tends to wilt.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Cut the stem open and look inside: it stays clean and white all the way through, with no brown staining, no dead crumbly center, and no ooze seeping out. The trouble stays on the leaves and fruit only.
